title: 🌋 Lake Nyos: How a Lake Suffocated 1,746 People in One Night
description: On August 21, 1986, a deep volcanic lake in Cameroon released roughly 1.6 million tons of carbon dioxide in a single belch. The invisible gas flowed downhill through villages, killing 1,746 people and 3,500 livestock as they slept. The Nyos disaster forced scientists to recognize a class of hazard they had never planned for. #LakeNyos #Cameroon #LimnicEruption #CO2 #DisasterDecode

segments.voice: On the night of August 21, 1986, Lake Nyos in northwest Cameroon emitted a roar that traveled for kilometers. Witnesses on its rim described a column of water and froth rising from the surface to fall back as a thick mist. A wind blew downhill from the lake. Within minutes, that wind reached the villages of Lower Nyos, Cha, and Subum. By morning, 1,746 people were dead. Their livestock — about 3,500 cattle, sheep, and goats — were also dead. Birds had fallen from the sky. The killer left no marks, no smell, no warning. It was carbon dioxide.
segments.visual: Lake Nyos Cameroon 1986 night villages aerial Lower Nyos Cha Subum disaster

segments.voice: Lake Nyos sits in a maar — a volcanic crater formed by an explosive interaction between rising magma and groundwater. The lake is about 1.9 kilometers long and 200 meters deep. The maar formed approximately 400 years ago and has been geologically dormant ever since. Below the lake bed, magma slowly seeps gases upward through fractures. Most of those gases are carbon dioxide, produced by the cooling and crystallization of magma several kilometers down.
segments.visual: Lake Nyos maar crater dimensions 200 meters deep volcanic geology underground

segments.voice: Under normal conditions, CO2 dissolves into the water of the lake. It is held in solution by the pressure of the water column above. The deeper the water, the more gas the water can hold. Lake Nyos's lower waters had been accumulating CO2 for centuries — possibly for the lake's entire 400-year existence. By the 1980s, the bottom layer was nearly saturated with dissolved gas, like a pressurized soda bottle held closed by the weight of water above.
segments.visual: CO2 dissolved bottom layer pressure water column saturation soda bottle analogy

segments.voice: Here's what nobody saw coming: a triggering event lifted a portion of that bottom water toward the surface. The exact trigger has been debated. Possibilities include a small landslide on the lake's underwater walls, a temperature inversion from cool rainfall, or internal density currents from a hot spell that summer. Whatever the cause, once gas-rich water rose by enough meters, the pressure dropped below the saturation point. Bubbles formed. The water fizzed.
segments.visual: trigger landslide rainfall density current gas-rich water rises bubbles form

segments.voice: The fizzing accelerated. As bubbles formed, they made the water less dense. Less dense water rose faster. As it rose, more gas came out of solution. More bubbles. More rising. Within minutes, a self-sustaining chimney of degassing water connected the lake bottom to the surface. The phenomenon has the technical name limnic eruption. Roughly 1.6 million tons of CO2 escaped from the lake in less than an hour.
segments.visual: limnic eruption chimney bubbles cascade self-sustaining 1.6 million tons CO2

segments.voice: Carbon dioxide is heavier than air. The cloud released from Lake Nyos did not rise into the atmosphere. Instead, it flowed downhill like water. Topography around the lake forms a series of valleys and saddles. The cloud followed those valleys, accelerating in some places, pooling in others. In valleys with low ridges trapping it, the gas concentration reached lethal levels. In open ridges, the cloud thinned and spread.
segments.visual: CO2 heavier than air downhill flow valleys saddles topography cloud movement

segments.voice: Death from CO2 asphyxiation is fast and silent. At concentrations above 10 percent, unconsciousness occurs within seconds. At higher concentrations, breathing reflex stops. Victims at Lake Nyos collapsed where they sat or slept. There was no struggle. The morning after, survivors who had been on high ground or who had heard warning shouts came down to find their families lifeless in the same positions they had occupied the night before.
segments.visual: CO2 asphyxiation 10 percent unconsciousness seconds silent death victims collapsed

segments.voice: The first emergency teams reached the area on August 22. They found bodies along roads, in courtyards, inside homes. They found bodies near rivers — animals that had come to drink during the night. They found a few survivors with chemical burns where exposed skin had touched the corrosive bicarbonate compounds in the gas mist. Cameroonian government rescue workers, military teams, and Red Cross personnel began the recovery while still uncertain what had killed everyone.
segments.visual: rescue teams August 22 1986 bodies courtyards homes burns survivors recovery

segments.voice: Within days, scientists from Cameroon, France, the United States, the United Kingdom, and several other countries arrived. The team included George Kling, a young limnologist who would become one of the central scientific figures in the Nyos story for the next four decades. Kling had been studying tropical lakes for his doctorate. His expertise on lake stratification and gas chemistry was suddenly relevant to a real-time disaster response.
segments.visual: international scientists 1986 George Kling limnologist tropical lakes investigation

segments.voice: What the scientists found in the months that followed transformed their understanding. The lake was still loaded with CO2. Even after losing 1.6 million tons in the eruption, the lower waters held perhaps 300 million cubic meters of dissolved gas — at 1 atmosphere of pressure, that volume of gas would fill a sphere over 800 meters across. Another limnic eruption could occur at any time. The villages had been resettled. They sat in the same path. They were vulnerable to the same disaster.
segments.visual: remaining CO2 300 million cubic meters second eruption risk villages resettled

segments.voice: The solution was conceived almost immediately by French engineer Michel Halbwachs and his colleagues. Insert a vertical pipe from the lake surface to the gas-rich bottom waters. Once water from the bottom is lifted past the saturation depth, gas comes out of solution naturally. The fizzing creates upward thrust. The pipe self-pumps. A small starter pump primes the system, and after that, gravity and physics do the work continuously. The technique is called controlled degassing.
segments.visual: Michel Halbwachs engineer vertical pipe self-pumping degassing solution diagram

segments.voice: The first degassing pipe was installed at Lake Nyos in 2001 — fifteen years after the disaster. Funding had been a long battle. The pipe was 200 meters long and could discharge gas-rich water at the surface, where the CO2 vented harmlessly into the open air. A single pipe took several years to make a meaningful dent in the dissolved gas inventory. Two more pipes were added in 2011. The combined system has now removed most of the dangerous bottom gas.
segments.visual: degassing pipe 2001 Lake Nyos 200 meters surface discharge installation engineering

segments.voice: The 1986 Nyos disaster was not the first limnic eruption documented. On August 15, 1984 — just two years earlier — a smaller event at Lake Monoun, also in Cameroon, killed 37 people. At the time, scientists had no framework to interpret what had happened. The Monoun deaths were locally treated as a witchcraft event or a poisoning. Only after Nyos did international researchers reanalyze Monoun and recognize the same physical mechanism.
segments.visual: Lake Monoun 1984 37 dead earlier event reinterpretation post-Nyos analysis

segments.voice: Lake Monoun was also degassed using vertical pipes. By 2008, the Monoun system was complete. The lake's residual CO2 inventory has dropped to safe levels. Monitoring continues. Cameroon's Institute of Geological and Mining Research, working with international partners, maintains regular sampling of both lakes. The data show steady reduction in dissolved gas. The risk of another sudden release is now considered low for both lakes.
segments.visual: Lake Monoun degassing 2008 IRGM Cameroon monitoring sampling reduction gas

segments.voice: But the tremor was just the start of the global hazard mapping. Are there other lakes like Nyos? The third candidate is Lake Kivu, on the border between Rwanda and the Democratic Republic of the Congo. Kivu is much larger — about 89 kilometers long and 480 meters deep. It contains an estimated 300 cubic kilometers of dissolved CO2 and 60 cubic kilometers of dissolved methane in its lower waters. The total gas inventory dwarfs Nyos by a factor of more than a thousand.
segments.visual: Lake Kivu Rwanda DRC dimensions 89 kilometers 480 meters CO2 methane comparison

segments.voice: Two million people live in the lakeshore region of Lake Kivu. The cities of Goma and Bukavu sit on its shores. A limnic eruption at Kivu would not just kill villagers in adjacent valleys. It would potentially kill hundreds of thousands of people across one of Africa's most densely populated regions. The Goma volcano Nyiragongo, which last erupted in 2021, sits next to the lake and provides an obvious source of triggering disturbance. The risk is real and recognized.
segments.visual: Goma Bukavu population two million Nyiragongo volcano 2021 eruption Kivu shore

segments.voice: Kivu's risk has been studied for decades. Researchers from Germany, Switzerland, the United States, and several African universities have made it among the most-monitored lakes in the world. The current scientific consensus is that Kivu is gradually approaching saturation but is not at immediate risk. The methane component creates an additional opportunity: methane can be extracted commercially as energy. Rwanda and the DRC have pursued projects to extract methane from Kivu, simultaneously generating electricity and reducing the gas inventory.
segments.visual: Kivu monitoring saturation methane extraction Rwanda electricity gas reduction

segments.voice: The KivuPower project, operated by ContourGlobal, came online in 2015 and has produced megawatts of electricity from Kivu methane. Each cubic meter of methane extracted reduces the total gas inventory of the lake. The economic incentive aligns with the safety incentive. It is one of the few examples in the world where a natural hazard is mitigated by industrial extraction at meaningful scale.
segments.visual: KivuPower 2015 ContourGlobal methane electricity industrial extraction safety

segments.voice: Lake Nyos itself is still actively monitored. CO2 continues to enter the bottom waters from underground sources at a rate of perhaps 5 million cubic meters per year. The degassing pipes vent more than that out. The net trend is downward. Permanent monitoring instruments measure dissolved gas at multiple depths. Real-time data are accessible to research teams. A repeat of 1986 is now considered extremely unlikely while the pipe system runs.
segments.visual: Nyos current monitoring 5 million cubic meters annual influx degassing balance

segments.voice: What remains of the affected villages? Lower Nyos, Cha, and Subum were largely abandoned after the disaster. Survivors were resettled in safer locations on higher ground. Cameroonian authorities at first banned return to the lakeshore, but over the years, some agricultural use has crept back. The dam at the lake's outlet — a natural feature, not constructed — was identified by engineers as fragile. Studies in the 1990s suggested it could fail in a strong earthquake, releasing the entire lake into downstream villages.
segments.visual: abandoned villages resettlement higher ground natural dam fragility downstream risk

segments.voice: Reinforcement of the natural dam was completed in 2019 by the Cameroonian government with international support. The work used soil-cement and rock fill to stabilize the spillway and reduce the risk of catastrophic failure. The combined effect of dam reinforcement and degassing means that Lake Nyos is now one of the safer locations along its valley system, after thirty-three years of risk reduction work.
segments.visual: dam reinforcement 2019 soil cement rock fill spillway stabilization completion

segments.voice: The Nyos disaster reshaped how scientists categorize natural hazards. Before 1986, hazard taxonomies recognized eruptions, earthquakes, tsunamis, and floods. Limnic eruption was added as a new category. Hazard maps for African lakes were redrawn. Volcanological surveys in Indonesia, the Philippines, and Central America inventoried crater lakes for similar conditions. Most fortunately turned out to be too shallow or too well-mixed to accumulate dangerous gas inventories.
segments.visual: hazard taxonomy expansion limnic eruption category global crater lake inventories

segments.voice: The Nyos team's longevity is itself notable. George Kling has visited the lake more than a dozen times across 35 years. Engineer Michel Halbwachs led the degassing pipe design and continued to manage the installation through retirement. Cameroonian researcher Joseph-Pierre Tchoua, of the University of Yaoundé, contributed local expertise from the early years onward. The continuity of these scientists has shaped what worked, what failed, and what was learned.
segments.visual: Kling Halbwachs Tchoua Cameroon Yaoundé scientific continuity decades work

segments.voice: What can the rest of the world learn? Slow-accumulating natural hazards exist in places where no one is watching. They can lurk in landscapes that look static. Local communities sometimes have knowledge that scientific institutions ignore — Cameroonian residents had reported strange events at Lake Monoun and Lake Nyos for years, attributed to spirits, before science caught up. Bridging local observation and technical analysis remains a challenge in many regions.
segments.visual: slow hazard accumulation static landscapes local knowledge science integration

segments.voice: Subscribe to DisasterDecode and hit the bell. Next week's disaster goes deeper into the Vajont dam — the Italian engineering project that knew the mountain was sliding and killed 1,917 people anyway in 1963.
segments.visual: Vajont dam Italy 1963 mountain slide 1917 dead engineering arrogance channel
